title: Model-based clustering of high-dimensional data: Pitfalls & solutions
speaker: David Dunson
tags: Virtual Workshop on Missing Data Challenges in Computation Statistics and Applications

proof_stages.label: High-dimensional clustering problem and desiderata
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the task of clustering massive-dimensional observations, especially gene-expression vectors, while retaining uncertainty quantification and avoiding purely algorithmic one-clustering outputs.

proof_stages.label: Bayesian mixture-clustering apparatus
proof_stages.summary: A model-based clustering framework is set up through mixture densities, Bayesian priors on weights or partitions, nonparametric Dirichlet/Pitman-Yor priors, MCMC data augmentation, and straightforward missing-data imputation.

proof_stages.label: High-dimensional posterior-failure question
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains why high-dimensional kernels are forced between over-simple and over-flexible parameterizations, motivating the question whether observed clustering degeneracies are MCMC artifacts or true posterior limits when p grows with n fixed.

proof_stages.label: Partition-posterior degeneracy theorem
proof_stages.summary: After expressing the posterior probability of a partition as prior mass times marginal likelihood, the main theorem shows that marginal-likelihood ratios for merging clusters tending to zero or infinity force the posterior to concentrate respectively on one cluster or on all singleton clusters.

proof_stages.label: Gaussian-kernel corollaries
proof_stages.summary: Applying the theorem to standard Gaussian location-mixture models shows that common parsimonious kernels produce singleton-cluster collapse, whereas more flexible full-covariance kernels produce one-cluster collapse, independently of the true data-generating distribution.

proof_stages.label: Latent factor mixture middle ground
proof_stages.summary: The proposed LAM model avoids both extremes by using a sparse factor model for the high-dimensional observations and placing a flexible mixture only on fixed-dimensional latent factors.

proof_stages.label: Bayes-oracle asymptotic justification
proof_stages.summary: A Bayes-oracle clustering posterior based on the true latent variables is defined, and under factor-model assumptions the LAM posterior over partitions is shown to converge to this oracle as p tends to infinity with n fixed; the later Q&A clarifies that this is not a separate separation-based consistency theorem.

proof_stages.label: Computation, empirical validation, and extensions
proof_stages.summary: The method is implemented with Gibbs and split-merge MCMC, compared against Gaussian-mixture, infinite-factor-analyzer, and sparse-PCA/k-means competitors in simulations and single-cell data, and discussed in relation to scalability and robustness extensions.

examples.name: Single-cell RNA-seq cell-type clustering
examples.note: Single-cell gene-expression data provide the motivating biomedical setting and later a benchmark for comparing LAM with Seurat and PCA/k-means, but the theoretical argument does not depend on this dataset.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses single-cell RNA-seq as the motivating application, visualizes it with UMAP, and later applies LAM/Seurat/PCA-k-means to a real dataset and compares ARI values against a gold standard

examples.name: Sparse PCA followed by k-means baseline
examples.note: The two-stage baseline exposes why unsupervised dimension reduction before clustering is unsatisfactory and motivates LAM as an integrated probabilistic analogue of sparse factor reduction plus clustering.
examples.engagement_reason: sparse PCA followed by k-means is not only proposed as a baseline but run in simulations and the real-data comparison, with ARI and estimated cluster counts discussed

examples.name: Spherical Gaussian location mixture
examples.note: This k-means-like Gaussian special case illustrates the theorem by showing that an overly rigid within-cluster kernel drives the posterior to singleton clusters as dimension grows.
examples.engagement_reason: the spherical Gaussian location mixture is analyzed as a corollary, specifying sigma squared identity and deriving the bad posterior limit of singleton clusters

passes.reason: The isotropic Gaussian mixture has spherical symmetry and is used to prove the bad singleton-cluster posterior limit.

The isotropic Gaussian location mixture is a maximally regular, collapsed subfamily used to exhibit the all-singletons posterior failure.

The isotropic Gaussian mixture is maximally symmetric and is used to show a bad posterior limit with singleton clusters.
spherical covariance is a collapsed maximally regular Gaussian submodel used to prove the singleton-cluster bad limit
the spherical Gaussian location mixture is used to show a bad posterior limit where every observation forms its own cluster, and its isotropic covariance gives maximal rotational symmetry
The highly parsimonious spherical location mixture is used to exhibit posterior failure to the all-singletons limit, and its covariance/mean structure is collapsed and maximally regular.
maximally regular spherical Gaussian location mixture is a minimal-complexity extreme used to show singleton-cluster posterior failure
Used to prove a bad all-singletons posterior limit for an overly parsimonious, maximally isotropic Gaussian mixture.
isotropic Gaussian location mixture is a maximally regular model used to show the singleton-cluster bad posterior limit
The overly parsimonious isotropic location mixture is used to prove singleton-cluster posterior collapse.

examples.name: Full-covariance Gaussian location mixture
examples.note: The conjugate full-covariance Gaussian mixture illustrates the opposite failure mode: excessive within-cluster flexibility makes the posterior collapse to one cluster.
examples.engagement_reason: the full-covariance Gaussian location mixture is analyzed via a conjugate inverse-Wishart/normal setup and shown to have the opposite bad posterior limit of one cluster

passes.reason: An otherwise standard full-covariance Gaussian location mixture is used to prove the opposite bad all-in-one-cluster posterior limit.

maximally flexible full-covariance Gaussian location mixture is used to show all-in-one-cluster posterior failure

examples.name: Challenging simulation scenarios
examples.note: Mixture-factor, LAM-generated, and zero-inflated Poisson-like simulation designs are used to stress-test clustering accuracy and cluster-count recovery under high dimension.
examples.engagement_reason: the challenging simulation scenarios are explicitly run and interpreted, including dimensions, true cluster numbers, ARI comparisons, credible intervals, and failure/success patterns
